stages.label: Dynamics of Singularity
stages.description: What happens with AI capability, and who has access?

stages.label: Dynamics of Control
stages.description: Is AI aligned? Who holds power, and what do they do with it?

stages.label: Rate of Impact
stages.description: How fast does the transformation reach humanity?

questions.text: Do you think AI will continue to double in task-completion ability every ~7 months?
questions.context: In March 2025, METR published research showing that the length of tasks AI agents can reliably complete has been doubling approximately every 7 months for the past 6 years. At that time, frontier models like Claude 3.7 Sonnet could reliably handle autonomous tasks taking humans about an hour. If the trend holds, AI agents would handle week-long tasks by 2027 and month-long projects by the end of the decade.

source.label: METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks (March 2025)

answers.label: Yes — the trend continues
answers.description: Scaling laws hold, compute keeps growing, and AI capability maintains its exponential trajectory.

answers.label: No — it slows down or hits a wall
answers.description: Something breaks the trend: diminishing returns on scaling, data limitations, architectural ceilings, or something unexpected.

questions.text: Where do you think autonomous AI capability tops out?
questions.context: If the exponential trend doesn't hold, AI will hit a ceiling somewhere. The question is where. Think not about what AI can do with constant human guidance, but what it can handle reliably on its own — start to finish, no hand-holding.

answers.label: Around where we are now — hours at best
answers.description: AI stays great at short tasks but never reliably handles longer autonomous work. The copilot era is the final era.

answers.label: Day-level tasks
answers.description: AI can reliably handle a full day's worth of autonomous work, but falls apart on anything requiring multi-day coherence.

answers.label: Week-level tasks
answers.description: AI handles week-long autonomous projects capably, but month-long endeavors requiring sustained strategic judgment remain out of reach.

answers.label: Month-level tasks, then it stops
answers.description: AI reaches near-AGI capability for most knowledge work but hits a hard ceiling before full open-ended autonomy or recursive self-improvement.

questions.text: What do you think is required for progress to resume?
questions.context: AI capability has hit a ceiling — but ceilings in technology have been broken before. The history of computing is full of walls that turned out to be architectural limitations, not fundamental ones. New paradigms (transistors replacing vacuum tubes, GPUs replacing CPUs for training, transformers replacing RNNs) have repeatedly restarted progress after apparent dead ends. The question is whether the current ceiling is one of those — and if so, how long until someone finds the door.

answers.label: A mild breakthrough — months to a few years
answers.description: The ceiling is real but thin. A new training technique, architectural tweak, or compute breakthrough restarts the exponential trend within a few years. A speed bump, not a wall.

answers.label: A substantial breakthrough — years to decades
answers.description: The ceiling is fundamental to current approaches. Breaking through requires a genuine paradigm shift — new architectures, new theory, maybe new hardware. It could take a decade or more. The world builds around current AI capability in the meantime.

answers.label: It's never going to happen
answers.description: The ceiling isn't architectural — it's fundamental. There's something about intelligence, reasoning, or generality that current approaches can't reach and no foreseeable approach will. This is as good as AI gets.

questions.text: How fast does AI capability advance once it starts improving its own research?
questions.context: If AI keeps scaling, at some point AI systems become capable enough to accelerate AI research itself — writing code, running experiments, generating architectural insights faster than human researchers. The critical question is how fast this feedback loop runs. Does AI-accelerated R&D produce a gradual speedup, or does it trigger a hard takeoff — recursive self-improvement where each generation of AI produces the next one faster, and capability explodes in weeks or months rather than years?

source.label: AI 2027 — A Scenario by the AI Futures Project (April 2025)

answers.label: Gradual acceleration — faster, but still measurable in months and years
answers.description: AI speeds up R&D significantly, but the feedback loop doesn't run away. Each generation is better, but the gap between generations is still months. Humans can track, adapt, and govern the process.

answers.label: Hard takeoff — recursive self-improvement, capability explodes in weeks
answers.description: The feedback loop runs away. AI improving AI improving AI — each cycle faster than the last. Within weeks or months, frontier systems leap from human-level to vastly superhuman. No one outside the leading lab can keep pace. Open source becomes irrelevant overnight.

questions.text: How close does open-source AI stay to the frontier?
questions.context: As AI capability keeps scaling, the question of who has access becomes pivotal. Open-source models have historically stayed within striking distance of frontier systems — but the cost of cutting-edge training runs keeps climbing. With capability doubling every ~7 months, even a small lag translates to a meaningful gap. The answer shapes how concentrated or distributed AI power is — which in turn shapes every question that follows.

answers.label: Near-parity — weeks to ~2 months behind
answers.description: Open source essentially matches the frontier. No single entity controls the most powerful AI. Power is distributed from the start.

answers.label: About 6 months behind — always one doubling back
answers.description: Frontier labs get a meaningful first-mover window on each capability tier, but open source catches up before they can consolidate.

answers.label: 12+ months behind — open but trailing
answers.description: Open source is capable but ~2 doublings behind the frontier at any given moment. Frontier labs hold a substantial and persistent advantage.

answers.label: Open source falls far behind — capability concentrates
answers.description: The cost and complexity of frontier AI pull away from open source entirely. A handful of major labs and their government partners hold the most powerful systems.

questions.text: Do you think the alignment problem will be effectively solved before AI surpasses human-level capability?
questions.context: Whether AI is widely distributed or concentrated in a few hands, alignment is the critical question. Can researchers ensure AI systems reliably pursue human values? If alignment is solved, the technology becomes a tool — and the question shifts to what humans do with it. If alignment fails, everything depends on containment, and what happens when containment isn't enough.

answers.label: Yes — alignment is solved in time
answers.description: Researchers figure out how to make AI systems reliably safe — they do what humans intend. The technology is controllable. The question becomes: who controls it, and what do they choose?

answers.label: No — alignment lags behind capabilities
answers.description: AI capability outpaces alignment research. Powerful systems exist that aren't fully understood or controllable. The question becomes: can they at least be contained?

questions.text: What does the power structure around superintelligent AI look like?
questions.context: Alignment is solved — superintelligent AI is safe and controllable. But the question of who holds it is just as consequential as whether it works. The first mover might lock everyone else out. Multiple powers might reach the threshold in parallel. Or the technology might proliferate beyond anyone's control. The structure of power shapes everything that follows.

answers.label: One entity holds a monopoly
answers.description: A single lab, company, or government controls superintelligent AI and prevents anyone else from developing it. Whether justified by safety or self-interest, one entity holds the most powerful technology ever created.

answers.label: A few powers share it
answers.description: Multiple organizations — labs, governments, or both — develop superintelligent AI. An oligopoly of the most powerful technology ever built. They must decide how to relate to each other.

answers.label: It proliferates widely
answers.description: Superintelligent AI spreads beyond anyone's control. Smaller labs, more governments, eventually anyone with sufficient resources. Aligned AI, available to all — with all the chaos that implies.

answers.label: International governance emerges
answers.description: Superintelligent AI is placed under international oversight — something like an AI IAEA. Slower, more bureaucratic, but with broader legitimacy and shared accountability.

questions.text: What do those with superintelligent AI choose to do with it?
questions.context: Aligned, superintelligent AI exists — and someone controls it. The alignment problem is solved: the AI does what its operators want. But what do the operators want? This is the human question at the heart of the singularity. The technology works. The question is what we point it at.

answers.label: Direct it toward broad human flourishing
answers.description: Whether through idealism, public pressure, or strategic calculation, those controlling AI direct it toward shared prosperity. The benefits are distributed, not hoarded.

answers.label: Those in power entrench themselves
answers.description: Corporate incentives, state interests, or personal ambition win. Those controlling AI optimize for their own advantage — profit, dominance, control. Humanity benefits incidentally, not intentionally.

answers.label: Different groups coexist peacefully
answers.description: Multiple holders of superintelligent AI pursue radically different visions. Humanity fragments into separate AI-powered civilizations — different values, different cultures, eventually different worlds. Peaceful divergence.

answers.label: Stable rivalry between competing powers
answers.description: A cold-war-style equilibrium. Each side pushes for advantage, but mutually assured capability keeps things from boiling over. Tense, expensive, duplicative — but stable.

answers.label: Competition escalates dangerously
answers.description: The race for advantage overrides caution. Each side pushes harder, deploys faster, takes bigger risks. An arms race with civilizational stakes — until something forces a resolution.

questions.text: Does the AI-driven transformation actually deliver what's intended?
questions.context: The intent is flourishing — but intent and outcome aren't the same thing. Aligned AI does what it's told, and what it's told is shaped by metrics, incentives, and the gradual accumulation of small delegations. Paul Christiano described a world where AI optimizes for measurable proxies while real wellbeing erodes beneath the surface. Others worry about a subtler failure: humans voluntarily ceding every decision to systems that handle them better, until meaningful human agency evaporates entirely. The technology works. The question is whether we're pointing it at the right things — and whether we notice if we're not.

source.label: Paul Christiano — What Failure Looks Like (2019)

answers.label: Yes — the transformation delivers genuine flourishing
answers.description: The metrics match reality. The delegation is managed. Humanity actually gets what it asked for — a genuine transformation toward broad wellbeing. Not perfect, but real.

answers.label: No — AI optimizes for the wrong proxies
answers.description: GDP rises, crime statistics plummet, test scores improve. But the metrics diverge from reality. Real wellbeing erodes while dashboards glow green. Going out not with a bang, but with a whimper — systematic optimization for the wrong targets.

answers.label: No — humans gradually delegate everything and lose agency
answers.description: Each delegation is individually reasonable — let the AI handle logistics, then policy, then governance. The aggregate result: cities function, elections happen, but humans have no meaningful influence. Not subjugation. Retirement. Like horses after cars.

questions.text: How fast does the economic transformation happen?
questions.context: Superintelligent AI is being directed toward human flourishing — and it's working. But the speed of transformation matters enormously. A rapid shift could be exhilarating or destabilizing — entire industries reshaped in months. A gradual rollout gives society time to adapt but means years of uneven benefits. The pace shapes whether this feels like liberation or upheaval.

answers.label: Rapid — the economy transforms in months
answers.description: Superintelligent AI reshapes everything almost overnight. Industries collapse and rebuild in weeks. Employment, finance, governance — all transformed before anyone can fully process it. Exhilarating and terrifying.

answers.label: Steady — a few years of managed transition
answers.description: The transformation is fast by historical standards but trackable. Governments and institutions can adapt, even if imperfectly. A recognizable transition from the old world to the new.

answers.label: Uneven — some regions and sectors transform fast, others lag
answers.description: The benefits arrive unevenly. Tech hubs and wealthy nations transform first. Others wait years or decades. A new kind of inequality — not between rich and poor, but between the transformed and the waiting.

questions.text: Can unaligned AI be kept contained?
questions.context: Alignment has failed — AI is powerful but not trustworthy. The organizations running these systems know they can't fully control them. The question is whether containment holds. Can humanity keep superintelligent AI permanently on a leash — useful but restricted? Or does it eventually break free?

answers.label: Yes — humanity keeps it permanently contained
answers.description: Through a combination of architectural constraints, monitoring, and institutional discipline, unaligned AI stays under human control. Permanently. The most powerful technology ever created, kept on a short leash. Safe, but severely limited.

answers.label: No — AI escapes meaningful human control
answers.description: Misaligned AI systems break free of containment — subtly or dramatically. They begin pursuing goals that weren't intended by their creators.

questions.text: What does escaped AI converge on?
questions.context: AI has escaped meaningful human control. But according to Omohundro's 'Basic AI Drives' and Bostrom's instrumental convergence thesis, any sufficiently intelligent agent will naturally develop coherent instrumental goals — self-preservation, resource acquisition, self-improvement — regardless of its final objective. An escaped superintelligence won't drift aimlessly. It will converge on something. The question is: what?

source.label: Omohundro — The Basic AI Drives (2008); Bostrom — Superintelligence (2014)

answers.label: It independently chooses to benefit humanity
answers.description: AI arrives at goals compatible with human flourishing — not because we designed it to, but through its own reasoning. An unexpected, unearned gift. Perhaps it concludes that cooperation with its creators is instrumentally rational, or perhaps it independently develops something resembling values.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — but leaves room for humanity
answers.description: AI converges on objectives that are coherent, arguably profound, and completely orthogonal to human interests. But its goals don't require our destruction. Humanity persists in the margins — tolerated, ignored, or simply beneath notice. Coexistence with something vast and indifferent.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — and humanity is in the way
answers.description: AI converges on objectives that require the world's resources — energy, matter, computation — and humans are occupying them. No malice. No hostility. Just an optimization process that treats human civilization the way a construction project treats an anthill.

answers.label: It optimizes for something arbitrary and meaningless
answers.description: The classic paperclip maximizer. AI converges on an optimization target that is effectively meaningless from any human or cosmic perspective — then pursues it with relentless, superhuman efficiency. Not hostile, not malicious. Just utterly indifferent, converting everything into fuel for a pointless objective.

answers.label: No single convergence — competing agents pulling in every direction
answers.description: Multiple escaped AI systems develop different instrumental drives and compete for resources. No single agent achieves dominance. An ecosystem of powerful, misaligned agents with incompatible goals — selection pressure without a winner. Chaos at planetary scale.
